parameters.root_cause: Deployment of payment-api v2.3.1 (deployed ~8 minutes ago) omitted the required environment variable DB_CONNECTION_STRING. DatabaseConfig.getConnection throws a NullPointerException (config.DatabaseConfig.getConnection:42) on startup, producing HTTP 500s in PaymentHandler (handler.PaymentHandler.processPayment:87) and health check failures. Service status shows 0 active DB connections and high error rate (92%), confirming the app cannot reach postgres-primary due to missing connection string rather than a DB outage.
parameters.recommendation:
Immediate (mitigation): Redeploy payment-api with the correct DB connection string sourced from the secrets store (set environment var DB_CONNECTION_STRING to the postgres-primary connection URI / mount Kubernetes secret payment-db-conn), or rollback to the previous known-good release (v2.3.0) to restore checkout. After redeploy, verify payment-api shows active DB connections and low error rate.
Short-term hardening: add startup validation to DatabaseConfig to fail fast with a clear error if DB_CONNECTION_STRING is missing, and make readiness probes fail until configured.
Long-term fixes: update CI/CD to ensure required secrets/env vars are injected (fail deployment if missing), add unit/integration test in the pipeline that verifies DB env var presence, and add an alert for config-related NullPointerExceptions during deploys. Also add a runbook step to verify secret permissions and automated smoke test to hit /health after deploy.

{"tool_name":"submit_resolution","parameters":{"root_cause":"postgres-primary (Postgres 12.7) is the root cause. An ad-hoc/analytics long-running query workload on postgres-primary has exhausted available DB connections and caused severe locking / long-running queries. inventory-service (v2.4.1) is timing out acquiring DB connections from its pool (observed symptoms: queued requests, connection acquisition timeouts, and very long query durations ~5–30s), which cascades to checkout-service p99 >5s. There were no recent deploys to checkout or inventory, indicating an upstream DB load issue rather than code regression. Error signatures include DB-side long-running backends and connection exhaustion (e.g. "FATAL: remaining connection slots are reserved" / repeated connection acquisition timeouts in inventory-service logs).","affected_service":"postgres-primary","recommendation":"Immediate mitigations (1–15 minutes):
1) On postgres-primary, run SELECT pid, age(now(), query_start), state, query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state <> 'idle' ORDER BY query_start DESC; identify and terminate (pg_terminate_backend(pid)) long-running analytics queries (>60s) that are consuming connections. 2) Temporarily increase postgres max_connections from 100 -> 200 to relieve immediate pressure (monitor closely) and restart only if required/allowed. 3) Add a short-term statement_timeout (5s) for the inventory-service DB user or set statement_timeout at the inventory-service JVM config to prevent single requests from monopolizing connections. 4) Reduce inventory-service DB pool size (e.g. from 50 -> 30) and set connection acquisition timeout to a finite value (eg 2s) so requests fail fast instead of queuing. 5) If available, redirect analytical queries to a read replica or standby; suspend ad-hoc analytics jobs until stable.
Medium-term (hours–days): deploy PgBouncer in transaction-pooling mode in front of postgres-primary, add missing indexes found in the slow query plans (index inventory_items on the queried columns), tune DB autovacuum, and add an extra read replica for reporting. 
Monitoring / prevention: add alerts for pg_stat_activity long-running queries, used_connections > 80% of max_connections, and inventory-service DB pool saturated; add a runbook with the above kill/scale steps."}}

parameters.root_cause: postgres-primary (Postgres 12.7) is the root cause. An ad-hoc/analytics long-running query workload on postgres-primary has exhausted available DB connections and caused severe locking / long-running queries. inventory-service (v2.4.1) is timing out acquiring DB connections from its pool (observed symptoms: queued requests, connection acquisition timeouts, and very long query durations ~5–30s), which cascades to checkout-service p99 >5s. There were no recent deploys to checkout or inventory, indicating an upstream DB load issue rather than code regression. Error signatures include DB-side long-running backends and connection exhaustion (e.g. "FATAL: remaining connection slots are reserved" / repeated connection acquisition timeouts in inventory-service logs).
parameters.recommendation:
Immediate mitigations (1–15 minutes):
1) On postgres-primary, run SELECT pid, age(now(), query_start), state, query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state <> 'idle' ORDER BY query_start DESC; identify and terminate (pg_terminate_backend(pid)) long-running analytics queries (>60s) that are consuming connections. 2) Temporarily increase postgres max_connections from 100 -> 200 to relieve immediate pressure (monitor closely) and restart only if required/allowed. 3) Add a short-term statement_timeout (5s) for the inventory-service DB user or set statement_timeout at the inventory-service JVM config to prevent single requests from monopolizing connections. 4) Reduce inventory-service DB pool size (e.g. from 50 -> 30) and set connection acquisition timeout to a finite value (eg 2s) so requests fail fast instead of queuing. 5) If available, redirect analytical queries to a read replica or standby; suspend ad-hoc analytics jobs until stable.
Medium-term (hours–days): deploy PgBouncer in transaction-pooling mode in front of postgres-primary, add missing indexes found in the slow query plans (index inventory_items on the queried columns), tune DB autovacuum, and add an extra read replica for reporting. 
Monitoring / prevention: add alerts for pg_stat_activity long-running queries, used_connections > 80% of max_connections, and inventory-service DB pool saturated; add a runbook with the above kill/scale steps.
